U.S. Strategic Oil Releases Buy Time - But Not Much More

Since the start of the war with Iran and effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. petroleum inventories have fallen at some of the fastest rates on record, acting a shock absorber to avert fuel shortages and limit price rises.

The United States has become the supplier of last resort, accelerating exports of crude and refined products to the rest of the world, making up for some of the oil lost from the Persian Gulf.

The government owned Strategic Petroleum Reserve has functioned as intended to forestall panic buying and hoarding, minimise the impact on the national economy, and allow additional time for diplomatic and military planning.  

But the rate of inventory depletion is unsustainable, which explains the increasing urgency to find a way to re-open the Strait through negotiations or the provision of military protection to tankers transiting the waterway ...

This is JPM EM's method for counting vessels that contributed to its claim of "200% of pre-conflict" levels.

Method: Take snapshots of AIS data for vessels on each side of the Strait via Bloomberg's MAP function once a day. Identify confirmed transits (vessels appearing on one side of the strait and then subsequently on the other), and exclude support vessels (e.g. tug, landing craft, platform supply). In this methodology, we may on occasions miss some shadow vessels (if they re-appear only days later or far away), but we are able to capture vast majority of shadow crossings that have been reported in other media.

Consumer prices rose 0.5% in May, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday, lifting annual inflation to 4.2% from 3.8%—the first 4%-handle increase in three years.

But the heat was mostly contained in energy: It accounted for more than 60% of the monthly damage, with gasoline jumping 7% from April and 40.5% over the year as the war in Iran chokes the Strait of Hormuz and drives oil prices higher.

If you strip out food and energy from the “core” CPI, it rose just 0.2% in May and 2.9% over the year, softer than expected and suggesting that the spillover was relatively limited. That’s the number the Fed actually watches, and it isn’t flashing the overheating signs that could force its hand.

Overheating was the fear over this print, not just from the Iran war but from AI. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee has warned that the AI boom could heat the economy before it delivers any productivity gains, as the costs of the build-out grow. China’s economy showed a version of this overnight, with wholesale inflation near a four-year high on AI-driven demand. But May’s consumer data doesn’t show this yet; the hot part is still the oil shock.

Yet an inflation rate above 4% makes a rate cut harder for new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh to justify, especially after a surprisingly strong May jobs report. The risk now is that the oil shock, like the previous supply shocks that have battered the economy since the pandemic, proves to be sticky.

It’s an awkward print for Warsh in particular. He was confirmed in May in the closest Fed chair vote in modern history, and critics accuse him of being installed largely because Trump expects him to cut rates. Warsh, for his part, has argued that productivity gains from AI will allow the economy to grow faster without spurring inflation, thereby enabling the Fed to lower borrowing costs. 

But the American public is showing signs of fatigue with five years of above-2% inflation. Consumer sentiment fell to a record low in May, marking its third straight monthly drop, with 57% of Americans reporting that high prices are eroding their finances. Wednesday’s print showed that wages rose just 3.4% over the year, meaning that 4.2% inflation is beginning to outpace workers’ pay gains as prices rise in gas, electricity, food, and medical care. 

The market has gotten the message. After May’s strong jobs report and Goldman Sachs pulling its 2026 rate-cut forecast, traders began pricing out a rate cut and pricing in a hike. As of Wednesday, the CME FedWatch tool priced a 63% chance of a quarter-point increase by October, a near-total reversal from the cut consensus that dominated Wall Street weeks ago.

Jeffrey Roach, chief economist at LPL Financial, expects the central bank to “remain on hold while removing any bias toward additional easing,” basically stripping out the signal that cuts are coming.

Goldman Slashes 2027 Brent Outlook to $80

Goldman Sachs cut its average 2027 Brent crude forecast to $80/bbl, from $85, pointing to robust non-OPEC supply growth and structural demand weakness, led by China’s EV push and efficiency gains.

It held Q4 2026 at $90/bbl, noting the Hormuz disruption’s price impact has been milder than expected thanks to softer demand.

Goldman is doubling down on the fundamentals over geopolitics thesis.

Even with ongoing Strait risks, they see supply flooding in and demand softening enough to cap longer-term prices.

Goldman Sachs just cut its 2027 Brent crude estimate to $80 per barrel.

The revision is based on two factors: stronger supply from non-OPEC producers and structurally weaker demand from China.

Goldman estimates that over 10% of China's demand decline becomes permanent as the country diversifies its energy mix away from oil.

This matters because China has been the marginal buyer driving global oil demand growth for two decades. A structural shift changes the long-term calculus for crude.

Brent recently dropped 6.2% in a single session. WTI fell 5.9%. These moves may reflect the market repricing not just short-term supply concerns, but longer-term demand uncertainty.

The contrast is notable: short-term volatility is getting headlines, but the bigger story is a major bank revising its multi-year outlook lower based on permanent demand destruction.

Worth watching whether other banks follow with similar revisions.

Short-term volatility, but the real story is structural demand - watch China.

$USO #Oil #GoldmanSachs #CrudeOil #Markets #FinTok #Investing

🛢️ Goldman Sachs Just Trimmed Its 2027 Brent Oil Outlook, What This Means for Energy Markets

In a notable shift, Goldman Sachs has lowered its forecast for the average 2027 Brent crude oil price to $80 per barrel, down from a previous estimate of $85.

This revision reflects a more cautious long-term view, driven primarily by expectations of robust global supply growth, particularly from non-OPEC producers, meeting softer-than-anticipated demand. Factors like moderating economic growth in key consuming regions, efficiency gains, and the ongoing energy transition appear to be weighing on the demand side, while ample inventories and renewed production capacity ease earlier tightness concerns.

Why This Matters:
For Producers & Energy Companies: A lower price trajectory could pressure margins, especially for higher-cost operators. It signals a return toward structural balance rather than prolonged scarcity, potentially capping upside for oil equities and related sectors into 2027.

For Investors: This aligns with broader market sentiment from institutions like the EIA, which also sees prices easing toward the high-$70s/low-$80s range as supply responses kick in. However, geopolitical risks (e.g., ongoing tensions in key chokepoints) remain wild cards that could spark short-term volatility.

Macro Implications: Cheaper oil supports consumer spending and helps tame inflation, but it may challenge oil-exporting economies and investment in new upstream projects.

Oil markets are notoriously cyclical, today’s bearish revisions can quickly flip with any supply disruption or demand surprise. Goldman’s move highlights how rapidly forecasts evolve amid shifting fundamentals.

What’s your take? 
Will we see $80 Brent hold as the new normal in 2027, or do you expect volatility to push prices higher on unexpected catalysts? Drop your thoughts below 👇

...anything that gets shipped, because $90 oil doesn't stay at the refinery. It moves through everything.

Goldman isn't panicking and isn't revising down. That's the signal. In a week where oil has been pulled in every direction by trade war fears and demand uncertainty, the bank looked at all of it and kept its number exactly where it was.

At $90 a barrel, petrol prices stay elevated. Not crisis-level, but well above the sub-$70 lows we saw earlier this year. The relief at the pump that drivers briefly felt doesn't fully come back.

The hidden pressure is on industries that run on diesel and freight. Supermarkets, logistics companies, manufacturers. Their input costs stay high. That feeds into prices on the shelf, quietly, over months.

Goldman's refusal to cut the forecast is essentially a bet that the world keeps consuming oil faster than it can be replaced by alternatives. That bet has been right for two years running.

text:
🟢 $QQQ $720.10 (+0.41%)
— Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz announced that a final agreed-upon text of a US-Iran peace deal has been reached, with Pakistan working to finalize next steps. This is a concrete geopolitical development that directly explains the sharp drop in WTI crude, which is down 3.66% to $84.50, as a diplomatic resolution would ease supply risk premiums tied to Iran. Gold is up 3.21% to $4,245.90 despite the risk-on tone, while VIX is falling 3.55% to 18.75, suggesting markets are broadly welcoming the news.

*New details of a 14-point draft Iran–US memorandum:*

Immediate and permanent ceasefire on all fronts, including Lebanon.

US commitment not to interfere in Iran’s internal affairs and to respect Iran’s sovereignty.

Full lifting of the naval blockade within 30 days.

US commitment to withdraw its forces from areas surrounding Iran.

Reopening of the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days under Iranian arrangements.

Suspension of sanctions on Iranian oil, petrochemicals, and related exports, with full access to the resulting revenues.

US and its allies to provide reconstruction plans for Iran worth at least $300 billion.

A 60-day negotiation period to reach a final agreement on nuclear issues and the complete removal of US primary and secondary sanctions, as well as related UN Security Council and IAEA measures.

Iran reaffirms its NPT commitment not to develop nuclear weapons.

During negotiations, the US will not deploy additional forces to the region or impose new sanctions.

Release of $24 billion in frozen Iranian assets during the 60-day negotiation period, with half made available before talks begin.

Establishment of a monitoring mechanism to oversee implementation of the agreement.

The final agreement will be endorsed by a UN Security Council resolution.

Final negotiations will begin only after the release of half of Iran’s frozen assets, suspension of oil sanctions, and lifting of the naval blockade. 

The final deal will focus solely on uranium enrichment, enriched material, sanctions relief, and Iran’s economic reconstruction.

Iran’s missile program and support for resistance groups are excluded from the agenda.

Source: Mehr

Mark, some GULF STATES were 🇺🇸 ALLIES long before ISRAEL. DUMBASS🤣🤣🤣

Per Iphone Super @Grok listing of US GULF STATES ALLIES:
——

Expanded list of Gulf/Persian Gulf states plus Israel, ordered roughly by the earliest significant US diplomatic relations, recognition, treaty, or formal engagement (focusing on trade, diplomacy, or strategic ties).

This includes the core Persian Gulf bordering nations (all have ocean/sea access and major trading ports) and Israel (Mediterranean ports, key regional ally).

“Gulf States” here broadly covers Persian Gulf littoral states with seaside trading ports connected to the Indian Ocean via the Strait of Hormuz/Gulf of Oman.

1.  1833 Oman — Treaty of Amity and Commerce (one of the earliest US treaties with any Arab state; trade ties from late 1700s). Oman has direct access to the Gulf of Oman/Arabian Sea and Persian Gulf (Musandam exclave). Major ports like Muscat.

2.  1931/1933 Saudi Arabia — Diplomatic relations ~1931–1933 (oil concessions). Deep oil-for-security alliance (Quincy 1945). Extensive Persian Gulf coastline and ports (e.g., Dammam, Jubail).

3.  1931 Iraq — Diplomatic relations established. Persian Gulf access via Shatt al-Arab; major ports like Basra/Umm Qasr. Ties have fluctuated (adversarial post-1958/1990/2003).

4.  1948/1949 Israel — US recognition immediately upon founding (1948); strong strategic alliance since. Mediterranean ports (e.g., Haifa, Ashdod); key regional partner, not a Persian Gulf state but included per request.

5.  1961 Kuwait — Diplomatic relations after independence. Persian Gulf ports (e.g., Shuwaikh, Shuaiba). Strong US security ties post-1991 Gulf War.

6.  1971 Bahrain — Diplomatic relations after independence. Island nation in Persian Gulf with major ports (e.g., Khalifa Bin Salman). Longstanding US naval presence.

7.  1971/1972 UAE — Diplomatic relations after federation. Major Persian Gulf ports (Jebel Ali, Khalifa – among world’s busiest). Strong economic/security partnership.

8.  1972 Qatar — Diplomatic relations after independence. Persian Gulf ports (Ras Laffan, Hamad). Growing defense and economic ties.

9.  Iran — Diplomatic relations in the 19th/early 20th century (pre-1979 revolution ties, including 1950s–1970s alliances via CENTO/Baghdad Pact); severed after 1979 Islamic Revolution. Extensive Persian Gulf coastline with major ports (e.g., Bandar Abbas). 

Relations adversarial for decades. 
Notes:
•  All listed nations border the Persian Gulf (or its outlets) or have major ocean/sea trading ports in the broader region, facilitating global shipping (oil, LNG, containers).

•  Israel is the outlier geographically (no Persian Gulf border) but fits the “ocean seaside trading port” criterion via the Mediterranean and has been a core US ally.

•  Post-1970s/1991, US ties deepened with most Arab Gulf states via bases, arms sales, and Gulf War coalitions, though relations with Iran and Iraq have been more conflictual.

•  Dates prioritize earliest formal diplomatic/trade milestones; “ally” status varies (strong with most GCC + Israel; strained with Iran/Iraq historically).

This reflects evolving US interests in oil security, trade routes, and regional stability. Let me know if you want more details on any!

SEMPER FI

Oil fell more than 5% on Iran deal expectations.

Most people see a geopolitical headline.

Markets see something else:

A lower probability of disruption in the Strait of Hormuz.

Energy prices are often a pricing mechanism for geopolitical risk.
Crude inventories in the Gulf are increasing as millions of barrels of crude oil a day bypass regulations in the Strait of Hormuz. Traders and ship trackers report the shift. $WTI

President Trump called for the capture of Kharg Island then a peace agreement hours later, whipsawing oil futures during Thursday’s trading session. WTI for August delivery, /CLQ26, settled at $86.16 per barrel, down 2.18 from previous settlement. 

Thursday’s WTI session opened with renewed threats against Iran, sending August futures as high as $91.62 per barrel. In a Truth Social post, President Trump said the U.S. would strike Iran that night and could take control of Kharg Island in the future. Hours later, he announced progress toward a Memorandum of Understanding, which pressured WTI to an intraday low of $84.30 per barrel.

Iran had not confirmed an agreement at the time of the announcement, with confirmation reportedly not coming until early Friday morning. The gap between headlines and confirmation has been a recurring feature of the conflict and may have contributed to reduced market confidence. The volatility also prompted the Senate Agriculture Committee to send a letter to the CFTC requesting a review of recent futures-market activity.

Kharg Island has remained a focal point for Trump given its importance to Iranian oil exports. Kharg is a small island in the Persian Gulf which processes ~90% of Iran’s oil exports, roughly 2.6 million bpd with capacity of 7 million bpd. The capture of this island would cripple Iran’s ability to get their oil to market, however, it would also prompt retaliatory strikes against US military personnel and other oil infrastructure in the region, further tightening global supplies.

A potential agreement may reduce some of the geopolitical risk premium, but supply normalization would likely take time. Inventories outside commercial storage would also need to be rebuilt. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve has declined by roughly 64 million barrels since the start of the year, to about 349 million barrels.

The latest EIA report showed a broad draw in petroleum stocks. Commercial crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, while gasoline inventories rose by 0.2 million barrels to 215.1 million barrels. Distillate inventories declined by 0.2 million barrels to 102.1 million barrels. Refinery inputs averaged 17.0 million barrels per day, up 80,000 barrels per day from the prior week, with refineries operating at 95.3% of capacity.

Technicals
WTI August crude broke through the 50-day simple moving average of 88.14 but trading well above the 200-day SMA. 

The 14-day RSI settled at 44.63% and indicates a weakening in price as it heads towards the oversold threshold of 30%. 

The directional moving index indicates a strengthening bearish trend. The ADX is low but rising at 11.58. The positive directional index is low and weakening at 17.81 and below the negative directional index of 23.26, which is rising. 
DATA FROM JP MORGAN & SCHWAB.

•Juli–September: Immer noch bullisch, solange der Hormus-Konflikt nicht gelöst ist. Preise könnten bei anhaltender Störung $100+ testen.
•Ab Herbst/Winter 2026: Mögliche Entspannung, wenn Verhandlungen Fortschritte machen und Lieferungen wieder anlaufen. EIA erwartet dann fallende Preise (durchschnittlich ~$95 für 2026 insgesamt, später tiefer).54
Fazit für deinen Short: Die nächsten 4–8 Wochen sind besonders gefährlich für Short-Positionen — hier könnte es teuer werden, wenn Inventories weiter schrumpfen. Danach hängt alles von geopolitischen Entwicklungen ab (Trump/Iran-Deals). Märkte sind volatil: Positive Nachrichten lassen Preise schnell fallen (wie kürzlich passiert).
Das ist kein Finanzrat — Märkte können unvorhersehbar reagieren. Überlege Stop-Loss, Hedging oder Positionsgröße anzupassen. Wenn du mehr Details zu Charts, spezifischen Kontrakten oder Alternativen brauchst, lass es mich wissen!

#OIL PRICES SLIDE OVER 4% TO TWO-MONTH LOW AS MIDDLE EAST TENSIONS EASE

🔹 Global crude oil prices fell sharply on June 12, 2026, with West Texas Intermediate Crude Oil (WTI) dropping more than 4% to a two-month low near $83 per barrel before recovering modestly toward $84.80.

🔹 Brent Crude Oil (Brent) also came under heavy selling pressure, slipping below the $87 per barrel mark as traders unwound geopolitical risk premiums.

🔹 The decline was driven primarily by signs of de-escalation in the Middle East, including the suspension of hostilities between Israel and Iran and growing optimism surrounding ongoing US-Iran diplomatic discussions.

🔹 Markets interpreted recent comments from Donald Trump and statements from Iranian officials as increasing the probability of a broader agreement that could stabilize regional energy flows.

🔹 The easing of tensions has erased a significant portion of the geopolitical premium that previously pushed crude oil prices above $100 per barrel during the height of regional uncertainty.

🔹 The move lower occurred despite supportive fundamental data, including recent draws in US crude inventories, indicating that geopolitical developments currently remain the dominant market driver.

🔹 Technical analysts note that crude oil has broken below several key support levels, with some market participants now watching the $78–$80 per barrel region as the next potential downside target if diplomatic momentum continues.

🔹 Lower crude prices are generally viewed as positive for oil-importing economies such as India, helping reduce inflationary pressures, improve trade balances, and support corporate margins across sectors including aviation, paints, chemicals, logistics, and consumer goods.

🔹 For Indian equities, sectors such as airlines, auto, FMCG, chemicals, and paints could benefit from softer energy costs, while upstream energy companies may face near-term pressure from weaker crude realizations.

🔹 Market Impact: Falling oil prices have strengthened risk sentiment globally, contributing to gains in equities, easing inflation concerns, and increasing expectations that central banks may gain greater flexibility on future policy decisions.

Nigeria's Crude Oil Production Hits 15-Month High.

Nigeria's crude oil output jumped to a 15-month high in May as Africa's top producer continues to ramp up production, data from the Nigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission (NUPRC) showed. 

Nigeria pumped 1.53 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude last month, the highest level since January 2025, as the African country now produces at its ceiling per the OPEC+ group's agreements, after years of failing to do so due to sabotage, oil theft, and oil spills.

text:
🇷🇺 Russia’s oil production has been declining for the sixth consecutive month 

In May, average daily production stood at 9.009 million barrels per day, the lowest level in more than a year.
This figure is approximately 370,000 barrels lower than the November peak (9.38 million barrels per day) and falls 690,000 barrels short of Russia’s quota under the OPEC+ agreement.
According to analysts, the decline coincides with an increase in attacks on Russian oil infrastructure.

This sustained drop in oil production reflects the cumulative impact of Ukrainian strikes on refineries and related infrastructure. The gap from OPEC+ quotas and year-on-year lows further strains Russia’s economy and budget, highlighting the strategic effectiveness of Ukraine’s long-range campaign against Russia’s energy sector.

Understanding America’s Energy Dominance
There’s  no question: Donald Trump—over the course of the past decade—has played  an enormous part. Indeed, in his first 100 hours this term he announced  a plan to ‘unleash American energy’ (White House).  But: None of this unleashing could not have happened without American  innovation via the shale revolution. Stephen Moore looks at a new study:  Thanks to the shale oil revolution, the United States tripled its  oil output in less than 15 years, even as the Malthusian doomsayers (and  then President Obama) were assuring us that we would soon be drilling  the last barrel of oil on American soil. Shale gas also helped break the  back of the OPEC cartel, which, for nearly half a century, had a knife  at the neck of the U.S. economy and could, by turning off the spigots,  plunge the U.S. economy into a recession. Now a study shows that  fracking shale oil and gas have made America rich. The University of  California, Berkeley’s Energy Institute at Haas — hardly a right-wing  source — has released blockbuster findings on the shale gas revolution:  “It may seem like a distant memory now, but as of the mid-2000s, U.S.  natural gas production had been flat for a decade, and the U.S. was  importing liquefied natural gas (LNG), with plans to import much more.  Then shale gas happened. Advances in hydraulic fracturing and horizontal  drilling caused U.S. natural gas production to increase significantly,  and the U.S. went from being a net importer of natural gas to being the  world’s largest exporter. We calculate that U.S. natural gas consumers  have saved $3.1-$4.3 trillion between 2007 and 2025, equivalent to  $164-$227 billion annually” (Washington Times).
